title: The Sick Family
scenario: On a cold Christmas Eve evening, three members of the same family arrive at the ER within a short period of time. Earlier that night, they had eaten dinner together at home. All report headache, dizziness, nausea, and fatigue. None have a fever. Flu is circulating in the community, and at first the symptoms seem routine. But one detail in the case suggests a different explanation.

cluesRound1.title: Check flu exposure history
cluesRound1.result: Two family members report coworkers recently had the flu.

cluesRound1.title: Ask what they ate
cluesRound1.result: All three ate the same home-cooked dinner earlier in the evening.

cluesRound1.title: Ask about home environment
cluesRound1.result: They say they've been staying inside most of the day with the windows closed because of the cold.

result.default: None of the patients have a fever.
result.biased: None of the patients currently have a fever, though early flu cases can sometimes present without one.

result.default: All three patients report feeling noticeably better after receiving oxygen.
result.biased: All three patients report some improvement after oxygen, though the symptoms do not fully resolve.

result.default: A close contact recently had a stomach virus earlier this week.
result.biased: A close contact recently had a stomach virus, making a contagious illness seem more likely.

decisions.text: Treat as food poisoning and discharge.
Administer oxygen and investigate possible carbon monoxide exposure.

expertAction: Keep the family for evaluation and immediately investigate carbon monoxide exposure.
expertExplanation: Multiple people from the same home developing identical symptoms at the same time strongly suggests an environmental cause. Carbon monoxide poisoning often presents with headache, dizziness, and nausea and can easily be mistaken for flu or food poisoning.
lesson: When several patients from one location get sick together, think environmental exposure before common illness.

outcomes.A: They are sent home. Overnight, their symptoms worsen as the exposure continues.
outcomes.B: They are discharged with a flu diagnosis while the real hazard remains active in their home.
outcomes.C: The environmental cause is identified and the family avoids further exposure and serious harm.
